title: 27_401_vs_403.jsx
descriptionMd:
## Mission: The Wrong Error Message for a Permissions Failure

An admin panel fetches a protected report. When a logged-in non-admin user accesses it, they see: **"Please sign in to continue."** But they're already signed in — the server returned **403 Forbidden**, not 401 Unauthorized.

The component maps any non-200 to a single 'please sign in' message, conflating two completely different authentication states. The user is confused because they're already logged in.

Fix the handler to distinguish between 401 (not authenticated) and 403 (authenticated but not authorised) and show the correct message for each.
traceback:
Scenario A — logged-out user:
  GET /api/admin/revenue-report  401 Unauthorized
  UI: "Please sign in to continue."  ← correct

Scenario B — logged-in user without admin role:
  GET /api/admin/revenue-report  403 Forbidden
  UI: "Please sign in to continue."  ← WRONG — user IS signed in

The user sees 'sign in' but they're already signed in.
They try signing in again (or think their session is broken).
Correct message: "You don't have permission to view this report."
correctOutput:
Scenario A — logged-out user:
  GET /api/admin/revenue-report  401 Unauthorized
  UI: "Please sign in to continue."

Scenario B — logged-in non-admin user:
  GET /api/admin/revenue-report  403 Forbidden
  UI: "You don't have permission to view this report."

Scenario C — admin user:
  GET /api/admin/revenue-report  200 OK
  UI: renders the revenue report JSON

options.label: Check `r.status` separately for 401 and 403 and show distinct messages
options.resultLog:
Scenario A — logged-out user:
  GET /api/admin/revenue-report  401
  UI: "Please sign in to continue."

Scenario B — logged-in non-admin:
  GET /api/admin/revenue-report  403
  UI: "You don't have permission to view this report."

Scenario C — admin:
  GET /api/admin/revenue-report  200
  UI: renders report
options.rationale: 401 and 403 are different authentication states that require different user actions. **401 Unauthorized**: the user isn't logged in (no session token, expired token). Action: redirect to login. **403 Forbidden**: the user is logged in but doesn't have the role/permission for this resource. Action: show a permission error — NOT a login prompt, because logging in again won't help. Conflating them breaks the user experience: users who are already signed in see a 'sign in' button that does nothing useful.

options.label: Redirect to `/login` for any non-200 response
options.resultLog:
Scenario A — logged-out user:
  401 → redirect to /login ← correct

Scenario B — logged-in non-admin:
  403 → redirect to /login ← wrong: user is already logged in
  Logs in again, comes back, hits /api/admin/revenue-report, gets 403 again,
  redirect to /login again → infinite redirect loop

Scenario C (if 500):
  500 → redirect to /login ← completely wrong
options.rationale: Redirecting to login on any non-200 is dangerous: a logged-in user who gets a 403 will be redirected to login, log in (already logged in, so they're taken back), hit the 403 again, and get redirected to login again — infinite loop. A 500 error redirecting to login is confusing and unhelpful. Only redirect to login on **401** (unauthenticated). For **403**, show a permission denied message. For **500**, show a server error message.

options.label: Use `r.ok` but show both codes in the error message: `HTTP ${r.status}`
options.resultLog:
Scenario A — logged-out user:
  401 → UI: "Error: HTTP 401. Please sign in if you are not logged in."

Scenario B — logged-in non-admin:
  403 → UI: "Error: HTTP 403. Please sign in if you are not logged in."

Marginal improvement (the user sees a different number) but:
- HTTP status codes are technical jargon meaningless to most users
- The 403 case still suggests signing in might help, which it won't
- Users shouldn't be told 'HTTP 401' — they should be told what to do
options.rationale: Showing raw HTTP status codes to end users is poor UX — most people don't know what 401 or 403 means. Saying 'Please sign in if you are not logged in' for a 403 (forbidden) case still misleads the user. The goal is: translate the status code into the **user action required**. For 401: sign in. For 403: contact admin or stop trying (signing in won't help). Branching on `r.status` and showing targeted messages is the correct approach.

hints: Look up the difference between 401 and 403. One means 'you're not logged in'; the other means 'you're logged in but not allowed'. Which one requires a login prompt?
Instead of checking `r.ok`, check `r.status === 401` and `r.status === 403` separately and show a different message for each. 401 → 'please sign in'; 403 → 'you don't have permission'.
recruiterReview: Correct. 401 vs 403 is a classic trap — the names are counterintuitive ('Unauthorized' doesn't mean unauthorised, it means unauthenticated). The redirect-on-any-error distractor is dangerous in production: 403 → /login → 403 → /login creates an infinite redirect loop. The raw status code option marginalises the UX. The pattern of branching on specific status codes and translating to user-friendly messages is the professional approach. ✅
